emergent_families.name: Substrate
emergent_families.gloss: Where the data lives and how it moves: local-primary residence, offline use, conflict-free background sync, lock-in-free migration, portable export, and federation over a published wire contract — the local-first storage/replication/interop layer.

emergent_families.name: Sovereignty
emergent_families.gloss: Who controls access and identity: owner-controlled access authority, privacy-by-default, confidentiality/encryption, explicit least-authority grants, self-controlled identity, authentication, and public/private visibility — the user's standing as the authority over their data.

emergent_families.name: Assurance
emergent_families.gloss: How the system proves its own behaviour as built: typed/declarative contracts, code-verified conformance, and a layered derive-and-extend spec — machine-checkable guarantees about the application itself.

emergent_families.name: Verification
emergent_families.gloss: How a relying party independently confirms data has not been altered: tamper-evident integrity via signing/hash-chaining — trust without trusting the holder.

emergent_families.name: Disclosure
emergent_families.gloss: Governance of the live act of sharing/egress: user-chosen channel, runtime capability negotiation, pre-send payload preview, informed consent, priority-ordered policy, auditable consent receipts, least-privilege/human-in-the-loop guards, and governed LLM/agent egress — runtime mediation of every outbound disclosure.

emergent_families.name: Adversary-modelling
emergent_families.gloss: Reasoning about who the system must defend against: explicit threat-modelling of the design — the prerequisite stance that scopes the guards in the other families.

canonical_behaviours.name: Local-primary data residence
canonical_behaviours.definition: The authoritative copy of the data lives on the user's own device/store, owned and controlled by the user.
merged_from: keeps a local-primary copy of the data on the user's device
keeps a user-owned data store the owner controls

canonical_behaviours.name: Offline operation
canonical_behaviours.definition: The application reads and works without a network connection.
merged_from: reads / works while offline

canonical_behaviours.name: Background sync with conflict-free convergence
canonical_behaviours.definition: Changes replicate in the background and concurrent edits converge without a central authority.
merged_from: changes sync in the background
concurrent edits converge without a central authority

canonical_behaviours.name: Provider migration without lock-in
canonical_behaviours.definition: The user can move to another provider/host while keeping their identity and data intact.
merged_from: user can migrate to another provider without losing identity / data

canonical_behaviours.name: Portable, machine-readable export
canonical_behaviours.definition: Data can be exported in a portable, machine-readable format and is portable across applications.
merged_from: data can be exported in a portable, machine-readable format
data is portable across applications

canonical_behaviours.name: Owner-controlled access authority
canonical_behaviours.definition: The data owner is the authority deciding who may access the data.
merged_from: the data owner decides who may access the data

canonical_behaviours.name: Privacy as default posture
canonical_behaviours.definition: Privacy is the default; data is not exposed unless deliberately shared.
merged_from: privacy is the default posture

canonical_behaviours.name: Confidentiality / encryption at rest and in transit
canonical_behaviours.definition: Data is kept confidential, e.g. encrypted, and the transport cannot read message contents.
merged_from: data is kept confidential (e.g. encrypted)
the transport mechanism cannot read message contents

canonical_behaviours.name: Typed / declarative contract for behaviour
canonical_behaviours.definition: Behaviour is expressed as a typed or declarative contract rather than implicit code.
merged_from: behaviour is expressed as a typed / declarative contract

canonical_behaviours.name: Conformance verified by code, not prose
canonical_behaviours.definition: Conformance is established by executable checks/tests rather than asserted in prose.
merged_from: conformance is verified by code rather than asserted in prose
behaviour is checked by an executable test

canonical_behaviours.name: Layered, derive-and-extend spec
canonical_behaviours.definition: The specification is layered so others can derive from and extend it.
merged_from: the spec is layered so others derive-and-extend it

canonical_behaviours.name: Tamper-evident integrity
canonical_behaviours.definition: Data carries tamper-evident integrity via signing or hash-chaining.
merged_from: data carries tamper-evident integrity (signed / hash-chained)

canonical_behaviours.name: Federation via published wire contract
canonical_behaviours.definition: Systems interoperate through a published wire contract / federation protocol.
merged_from: systems interoperate via a published wire contract / federation
systems interoperate via a published wire contract

canonical_behaviours.name: Explicit least-authority grants
canonical_behaviours.definition: Access requires an explicit grant scoped to least authority.
merged_from: access requires an explicit grant (least authority)

canonical_behaviours.name: Self-controlled, host-independent identity
canonical_behaviours.definition: The user holds a self-sovereign identity independent of any host.
merged_from: user holds a self-controlled identity independent of any host

canonical_behaviours.name: Authentication required for access
canonical_behaviours.definition: Access requires authenticating the requester.
merged_from: access requires authentication

canonical_behaviours.name: Public/private visibility distinction
canonical_behaviours.definition: Data distinguishes public from private visibility states.
merged_from: data distinguishes public vs private visibility

canonical_behaviours.name: User-chosen outbound channel
canonical_behaviours.definition: The user chooses the channel used for outbound sharing.
merged_from: the user chooses the channel for outbound sharing

canonical_behaviours.name: Runtime capability negotiation/discovery
canonical_behaviours.definition: Capabilities are negotiated or discovered at runtime.
merged_from: capabilities are negotiated / discovered at runtime

canonical_behaviours.name: Pre-send payload preview
canonical_behaviours.definition: The user previews the exact payload before it is sent.
merged_from: the user previews the exact payload before it is sent

canonical_behaviours.name: Informed consent before disclosure
canonical_behaviours.definition: The user is shown what is disclosed and consents before any disclosure.
merged_from: the user is shown what is disclosed and consents before disclosure

canonical_behaviours.name: Priority-ordered policy
canonical_behaviours.definition: Policy rules are evaluated in an explicit priority order.
merged_from: policy is priority-ordered

canonical_behaviours.name: Auditable record / consent receipt
canonical_behaviours.definition: The system keeps an auditable record or consent receipt of disclosures and actions.
merged_from: the system keeps an auditable record / consent receipt

canonical_behaviours.name: Runtime least-privilege / human-in-the-loop guards
canonical_behaviours.definition: Runtime guards enforce least-privilege and require a human in the loop.
merged_from: runtime guards enforce least-privilege / human-in-the-loop

canonical_behaviours.name: Threat-modelled design
canonical_behaviours.definition: The design is explicitly threat-modelled.
merged_from: the design is threat-modelled

canonical_behaviours.name: Governed LLM/agent egress
canonical_behaviours.definition: An LLM or agent call over the data is governed like any other egress.
merged_from: an LLM / agent call over the data is governed like any other egress

leaf_behaviours: Exception as a first-class, stable-ID'd honest-deviation construct that flips a machine-readable mode bit. A PNA may deliberately depart from a named guarantee (an AC, or the local-only definition itself) by raising an Exception (EX-*); doing so sets pna-active=false (it is 'not a PNA right now') while remaining 'exception-handling conformant' if it is consented-to before the raise, persistently signalled, reversibility-declared, and per-dimension strength-disclosed (handler contract EX-H1..EX-H8). The raise/catch/handle software-exception analogy and the relying-party-keys-on-pna-active predicate split are not captured by any canonical behaviour (B21/B23 touch consent/receipts but not mode-suspension) and appear in no other reference in the incidence.

Constraint as the dual of Exception: a platform-imposed ceiling (CST-*) that the PNA must declare honestly rather than over-claim. The PNA stays conformant by reducing capability to what the platform can keep and declaring the frontier truthfully; over-reach (promising a capability the substrate cannot keep) is a *silent conformance failure*, the dual of an undeclared exception. No canonical behaviour captures honest platform-ceiling declaration, and no other reference engenders it.

Per-dimension strength-profile disclosure with refusal-to-collapse. Conformance is reported per AC/EX ID against a fixed assurance vocabulary (enforced / verifiable / best-effort / provider-asserted / recoverable-only / none) and a single rolled-up grade is explicitly forbidden (EARL-style per-clause reporting; 'checked, not awarded'; 'detect, don't bless'). The meta-honesty of refusing to confer a collapsed conformance grade is not any canonical behaviour and is absent from every other reference.

Harden flow: advisory governance of the *operating environment* the app runs in, treated as a sibling to (not a kind of) the app's own guarantees. Against an adversary in the runtime (an OS-level same-UID AI agent, another local process reading the store) the toolkit *advises* environmental countermeasures it cannot itself enforce (run under a separate OS user, sandbox/denyRead the store, MCP access broker, honeytoken+watchdog), with the same-UID-agent confinement analysis (ptrace/namespace/MAC) made explicit. B25 captures threat-modelling but not this advisory-not-mandatory environmental-countermeasure posture; no other reference engenders it.
pna.conjunction_readoff:
No reference in the incidence matches PNA's full family span together with its domain and conformance model. PNA's signature is the simultaneous combination of: direct-personal-network domain + strong Sovereignty + strong Assurance + strong Disclosure + strong Adversary-modelling, under a checked-not-awarded conformance model. That intersection is unoccupied in the corpus.

The corpus splits the requirements across disjoint clusters: (a) the direct-personal-network entries (Nostr, Matrix, ActivityPub, DSNP, Monica, Clay/Mesh, Dex, Folk/Cloze, IndieWeb, the contact RFCs, DXOS Contacts example) all carry Assurance none/partial and Disclosure none/partial — they are protocols/apps, not spec-conformance + egress-governance frameworks; (b) the strong-Assurance 'conformance verified by code' cluster (GitHub Spec Kit, Kiro, BMAD, OpenSpec, Tessl, Cucumber, LoRe, AgentRFC) is uniformly domain-general/distant with Substrate and Sovereignty none; (c) the strong-Disclosure+strong-Adversary cluster (AIUC-1, OWASP AISVS, ISO 31700-1) is general/adjacent AI-security or privacy-by-design, not local-first personal-network.

Closest single neighbour by the most distinctive axes: the DXOS Contacts example app on ECHO/Automerge+HALO — it shares the direct-personal-network domain, strong Sovereignty, strong Substrate (the local-first root), and strong Verification. But it has Assurance none and Disclosure none, so it lacks PNA's entire conformance-checking apparatus and its egress/consent governance — it matches barely half of PNA's span. On the conformance-method axis the nearest is the spec-kit cluster (checked, code-verified, derive-and-extend), and on the egress-governance axis OWASP AISVS / AIUC-1 — but each of those is domain-general with no Substrate/Sovereignty. PNA is the only artifact that conjoins all three.

reconciliation:
The emergent families DO NOT cleanly match the hypothesised four (Substrate, Sovereignty, Assurance, Verification). The honest result is: two of the four hold strongly, the third is thin, the fourth nearly collapses, and a large fifth family emerges that the four-way scheme does not name.

What matches: Substrate (B01-B05, B13) and Sovereignty (B06-B08, B14-B17) are robust, well-populated clusters that survive the merge intact and correspond closely to the hypothesis.

Where it strains:
1) ASSURANCE and VERIFICATION blur together and are both thin. The hypothesis treats them as peer families, but the corpus splits the assurance idea by *who is checking*: Assurance-as-self-proof (typed contracts, code-verified conformance, layered spec — B09/B10/B11) is about the builder demonstrating its own behaviour, while Verification-as-independent-confirmation reduces to a SINGLE behaviour, tamper-evident integrity (B12). A family of one is a sign the hypothesised Assurance/Verification split is finer than the evidence supports. A defensible alternative is to MERGE them into one "Assurance/Verification" family (B09-B12): checkable guarantees, whether self-attested or relying-party-confirmed.

2) A genuinely DISTINCT fifth family emerges that the four do not capture: DISCLOSURE / runtime egress governance (B18-B24, B26). This is the largest cluster after Substrate+Sovereignty and is qualitatively different from all four hypothesised families — it is neither about where data rests (Substrate), nor static control posture (Sovereignty), nor proving built behaviour (Assurance/Verification). It governs the *live moment of sharing*: preview, consent, receipts, priority-ordered policy, human-in-the-loop guards, and treating LLM/agent calls as egress. This strongly corresponds to the prompt's flagged candidate "a distinct honest-deviation / exception-handling family" — the runtime mediation/consent surface. It should be ADDED as a first-class family; folding it into Sovereignty would lose the build-time-vs-runtime distinction that the corpus clearly draws.

3) Threat-modelling (B25) is a stance/prerequisite rather than a behaviour peer; it sits adjacent to Disclosure and Sovereignty (it scopes their guards) and is reported as a small Adversary-modelling family rather than forced into one of the four.

Net recommendation: keep Substrate and Sovereignty; collapse Assurance+Verification into one checkability family (the Verification-as-separate hypothesis is not supported — it is a singleton); ADD a Disclosure/runtime-egress-governance family. So the four-family hypothesis is better described as: Substrate, Sovereignty, Assurance/Verification (merged), and Disclosure (added) — four again, but a different four than hypothesised, with threat-modelling as a thin cross-cutting fifth."
